nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers and streams with dense vegetation, soft acidic water, and leaf litter

diet.notes: Accepts quality dried foods readily; benefits from regular offerings of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia, Artemia, and mosquito larvae for optimal coloration and conditioning for breeding.

avoidWith: fish large enough to eat them

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke; 1024x683. Striking male on black background with full trident caudal fin, iridescent blue-green lateral stripe, blue-green iris and elongated dorsal/anal filaments — whole fish sharp and in frame.
Sven Kullander (SOK); 7360x4912. Tack-sharp lateral profile clearly showing the diagnostic trident caudal extension, dark lateral band, blue iris and yellow fins.
Krzysztof Bartosik (7TP); 6016x4000. Vibrant male in a planted tank with dramatic elongated dorsal/anal fin filaments and purple-blue iridescent sheen.

summary: A striking Colombian tetra prized for the males' iridescent purple sheen and extended trident-shaped caudal fin. Generally considered a beginner-friendly schooling fish that does well in soft, slightly acidic, planted community tanks.
careNotes: Males develop spectacular elongated dorsal, anal, and caudal fin filaments — the caudal rays extend to form a distinctive trident shape — along with a vivid purple-blue iridescent body sheen. Females have shorter fins and a more yellow-brown body tone, and tend to display greener irises while males typically show blue. Males can be mildly territorial toward one another, so groups of 6 or more with a skewed female ratio tend to reduce conflict. The closely related Rainbow Emperor Tetra (Nematobrycon lacortei) is sometimes confused in the trade; N. lacortei has a rainbow-iridescent body and lacks the trident caudal extension. Best kept in a well-planted aquarium with dark substrate and subdued lighting to bring out their coloration. Slow current preferred. Regular partial water changes (approximately 25% every 1–2 weeks) help keep them in good health.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers that generally breed without much difficulty in a well-conditioned aquarium. Conditioning with live or frozen foods and providing fine-leaved plants or spawning mops helps trigger spawning. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 1–5) and temperatures around 25–26°C are typically recommended for breeding. Parents tend to eat eggs, so removing adults after spawning or using a separate breeding tank is advisable. Fry are small and initially require infusoria or finely powdered foods before graduating to baby brine shrimp.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source; Aquarium Co-Op URL 404'd so SeriouslyFish used as primary. Covers taxonomy, parameters, tank size, breeding, and compatibility in detail.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 4.2 cm SL confirmed) and temperature/pH ranges. FishBase listed family as Acestrorhamphidae which appears to be a database error; Characidae confirmed by SeriouslyFish and wider taxonomic consensus.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size, temperature, pH, lifespan (~5 years), and compatibility. Size claim of ~2–2.5 inches (5–6.3 cm) appears overstated relative to FishBase and SeriouslyFish; averaged toward the more authoritative 3.5–4.5 cm range.

fish.breedingNotes: Emperor Tetras are egg-scatterers that generally breed without great difficulty once well-conditioned. Soft, acidic water and fine-leaved plants or spawning mops are recommended to trigger spawning. Parents will consume eggs if left in the breeding tank.
